memes.label: "Arno River"
memes.description: "The Arno River flowing through Florence — a natural boundary, a site of commerce and death, and a recurring symbolic presence throughout the novel."

memes.label: "Badia Fiorentina"
memes.description: "The Badia Fiorentina, the ancient abbey church whose graceful spire dominates the Florentine skyline in the Proem and whose sanctuary provides refuge to Romola in her hour of crisis."

memes.label: "Baldassarre Calvo"
memes.description: "Tito Melema's adoptive father, a Greek scholar enslaved and then abandoned by Tito, whose quest for vengeance drives one of the novel's central plot lines."

memes.label: "Bardi Family"
memes.description: "The Bardi family — an ancient Florentine noble banking house, patrons of learning, and the family of the blind scholar Bardo de' Bardi and his son Dino."

memes.label: "Bardo de' Bardi"
memes.description: "Bardo de' Bardi, the blind Florentine scholar whose devotion to classical learning and dependence on his daughter Romola form the emotional centre of the novel's opening Books."

memes.label: "Bargello"
memes.description: "The Bargello, Florence's prison and judicial palace, where Baldassarre is confined and where Romola witnesses the harrowing execution of Savonarola's supporters."

memes.label: "Bernardo del Nero"
memes.description: "Messer Bernardo del Nero, Romola's godfather and a prominent Florentine politician whose Medicean loyalties place him at the centre of the novel's political crisis."

memes.label: Bernardo Rucellai
memes.description: Florentine scholar-politician and host of the Oricellari Gardens circle, a centre of Renaissance intellectual life.

memes.label: "Betrayal in Romola"
memes.description: "The theme of betrayal — personal, political, and moral — as the engine of tragedy in George Eliot's Romola, centred on Tito Melema's escalating treachery."

memes.label: "Bonfire of the Vanities"
memes.description: "The mass burning of books, art, cosmetics, and luxury goods in Florence's Piazza della Signoria on 7 February 1497 — the climax of Savonarola's campaign of moral purification."

memes.label: "Bratti Ferravecchi"
memes.description: "Bratti Ferravecchi, a Florentine ironsmith and old-iron dealer whose streetwise practicality and sharp tongue make him a vivid figure of the Mercato Vecchio."

memes.label: "Careggi"
memes.description: "Careggi, the Medici villa in the hills above Florence, where Lorenzo de' Medici died in April 1492 — the event that opens the novel's political drama."

memes.label: "Catholic Church in Romola"
memes.description: "The institutional Catholic Church — the papacy of Alexander VI, the college of cardinals, and the ecclesiastical hierarchy — as a political and moral force in George Eliot's Romola."

memes.label: "Charity in Romola"
memes.description: "The theme of charity — from conventional almsgiving to transformative compassion — as the moral destination of Romola's spiritual journey in George Eliot's novel."

memes.label: "Classical Scholarship"
memes.description: "The study and reverence of classical Greek and Latin texts, a central intellectual theme in George Eliot's Romola"

memes.label: "Compagnacci"
memes.description: "The Compagnacci (Evil Companions) — a band of young Florentine aristocrats led by Dolfo Spini, violently opposed to Savonarola, and instrumental in his downfall."

memes.label: Concepts Index
memes.description: Index of all conceptual and thematic pages in the Romola wiki, from Renaissance humanism to religious faith.

memes.label: "Death in Romola"
memes.description: "The presence of death — through plague, execution, and natural mortality — as the horizon against which the moral drama of George Eliot's Romola unfolds."

memes.label: "Dino de' Bardi"
memes.description: "Dino de' Bardi (Fra Luca), Romola's elder brother who abandoned classical scholarship for the Dominican order, whose deathbed vision and crucifix transmit a supernatural warning to Romola."

memes.label: "Dino's Crucifix"
memes.description: "Dino de' Bardi's ivory crucifix — a small religious artwork that passes from the dying Dominican friar to his sister Romola, is stolen and sold by Tito, and becomes a recurring symbol of faith and betrayal."

memes.label: Dolfo Spini
memes.description: Leader of the Compagnacci, the anti-Savonarola youth gang in late 15th-century Florence.

memes.label: "Domenico Cennini"
memes.description: "Domenico Cennini, a Florentine goldsmith and member of the distinguished Cennini family of artisans, who purchases Tito's antique ring and provides his first financial foothold in Florence."

memes.label: "The Duomo"
memes.description: "The Cathedral of Santa Maria del Fiore in Florence — the religious and civic heart of the city, and the setting for many of the novel's most dramatic public moments."

memes.label: "Duty in Romola"
memes.description: "The theme of duty — filial, marital, and civic — as the moral framework through which Romola navigates her choices in George Eliot's novel."

memes.label: "Entry of Charles VIII into Florence"
memes.description: "The entry of King Charles VIII of France into Florence in November 1494 — a seismic political event that triggered the expulsion of the Medici, the establishment of the popular government, and Savonarola's ascendancy."

memes.label: Events Index
memes.description: Index of all historical and narrative events in the Romola wiki, from the Bonfire of the Vanities to Savonarola's trial.

memes.label: "Family in Romola"
memes.description: "The theme of family — as bond, as burden, and as legacy — shaping the choices and identities of characters in George Eliot's Romola."

memes.label: "Fear in Romola"
memes.description: "The role of fear — as psychological driver, political instrument, and religious emotion — in shaping behaviour and motivating action in George Eliot's Romola."

memes.label: Featured Pages
memes.description: Featured and premium pages in the Romola wiki — the highest-quality entries selected for flagship display.

memes.label: "Fiesole"
memes.description: "Fiesole, the hill town above Florence crowned with monastic walls, home to a Dominican monastery where the dying Fra Luca retreats and Tito's secrets are first threatened."

memes.label: "Florence"
memes.description: "The primary setting of Romola: the Renaissance city of Florence in the 1490s, during the tumultuous years following the death of Lorenzo de' Medici."

memes.label: Florence Landmarks
memes.description: Landmark places in Florence featured in the Romola wiki, from the Duomo to the Palazzo Vecchio.

memes.label: "Florentine Political Factions"
memes.description: "The political factions of Savonarolan Florence — Piagnoni, Arrabbiati, and others — whose rivalries shaped the city's turbulent politics in George Eliot's Romola."

memes.label: "Florentine Pestilence and Famine"
memes.description: "The pestilence and famine that struck Florence in 1497, creating widespread suffering and providing the setting for Romola's transformation into a figure of active charity."

memes.label: "Florentine Signoria"
memes.description: "The governing council of the Florentine Republic — the city's supreme executive body during the period of the novel."

memes.label: "Franciscan Order"
memes.description: "The Franciscan Order (Frati Minori) in Renaissance Florence — rival religious order to the Dominicans, based at Santa Croce, and central to the Trial by Fire of Savonarola."

memes.label: "Establishment of the Great Council"
memes.description: "The establishment of the Great Council in December 1494 — Florence's experiment in popular government on the Venetian model, championed by Savonarola as the political expression of his reform programme."

memes.label: "Grief in Romola"
memes.description: "The experience of grief — personal loss, public mourning, and the sorrow that pervades George Eliot's Romola as both psychological reality and narrative atmosphere."

memes.label: "Justice in Romola"
memes.description: "The theme of justice — divine, civic, and personal — as a structuring moral force in George Eliot's Romola, where every betrayal meets its reckoning."

memes.label: "Lorenzo de' Medici"
memes.description: "Lorenzo de' Medici (1449–1492), 'il Magnifico,' the de facto ruler of Florence whose death on the novel's opening day sets the political crisis of the narrative in motion."

memes.label: "Lorenzo Tornabuoni"
memes.description: "Lorenzo Tornabuoni, a young Florentine nobleman of distinguished presence and Medicean sympathies, whose politeness masks the factional loyalties that will lead to his execution."

memes.label: "Marriage in Romola"
memes.description: "The institution of marriage — as social contract, moral crucible, and narrative engine — at the heart of George Eliot's Romola."

memes.label: "House of Medici"
memes.description: "The dominant political and banking dynasty of fifteenth-century Florence, whose expulsion in 1494 sets the political stage for the novel's events."

memes.label: "Mercato Vecchio"
memes.description: "The Mercato Vecchio (Old Market), the central marketplace of Renaissance Florence and a key setting in the novel's opening chapters."

memes.label: "Monna Brigida"
memes.description: "Monna Brigida, Romola's loquacious cousin whose garrulous affection and worldly pragmatism provide comic relief and domestic texture in the Bardi household."

memes.label: Most Referenced Pages
memes.description: Statistical ranking of the most-referenced entry pages in the Romola wiki, ordered by total cross-references.

memes.label: "Nello"
memes.description: "Nello the barber, a garrulous and witty Florentine whose shop serves as a social hub and whose sharp observations introduce Tito Melema to Florentine society."

memes.label: "Nonnus's Dionysiaca"
memes.description: "The Dionysiaca of Nonnus of Panopolis — a fifth-century Greek epic poem in 48 books, comprising the most extensive surviving account of the myths of Dionysus."

memes.label: "Ognissanti"
memes.description: "The church and piazza of Ognissanti in western Florence, where Tito conducts covert business and the Umiliati (Humbled Brethren) maintain their charitable traditions."

memes.label: "Oltrarno"
memes.description: "Oltrarno, the district of Florence lying south of the Arno River, home to the Via de' Bardi, the Pitti Palace, and the poorer quarters where Baldassarre finds refuge."

memes.label: Organizations Index
memes.description: Index of all organizations in the Romola wiki, covering religious orders, political bodies, and family houses of Renaissance Florence.

memes.label: "Otto di Guardia"
memes.description: "The Otto di Guardia (Eight of Guard) — Florence's chief criminal magistracy, responsible for internal security, criminal justice, and the trial of Savonarola."

memes.label: "Palazzo Vecchio"
memes.description: "The Palazzo Vecchio (Palace of the People), seat of Florentine government under the Signoria, and the centre of political power in the novel."

memes.label: Persons Index
memes.description: Complete index of all person pages in the Romola wiki, with birth and death years and affiliations.

memes.label: "Piazza della Signoria"
memes.description: "The Piazza della Signoria, Florence's principal civic square and the stage for the Bonfire of the Vanities, political processions, and public spectacle in the novel."

memes.label: "Piero di Cosimo"
memes.description: "Piero di Cosimo (1462–1522), Florentine painter known for his eccentric personality and fantastical imagery, who appears as a sharp-eyed observer in the novel."

memes.label: "Pisa"
memes.description: "Pisa, the rival Tuscan city-republic whose revolt against Florence forms a crucial political backdrop to the events of Romola."

memes.label: "Palazzo Pitti"
memes.description: "The Pitti Palace, the grandiose Oltrarno residence begun by Messer Luca Pitti, whose colossal scale the Florentine Spirit marvels at in the Proem."

memes.label: Places Index
memes.description: Index of all places in the Romola wiki, spanning the streets and churches of Renaissance Florence to the broader Italian landscape.

memes.label: "Poliziano's Miscellanea"
memes.description: "Angelo Poliziano's Miscellanea (1489) — the work of classical philology that Romola is shown reading aloud to her blind father in Chapter V."

memes.label: "Ponte Vecchio"
memes.description: "The Ponte Vecchio (Old Bridge), the iconic bridge spanning the Arno, a recurring setting for crossings, encounters, and moments of symbolic transition in the novel."

memes.label: "Power Dynamics in Romola"
memes.description: "Analysis of the triangular power structure in George Eliot's Romola: institutional power (Florence), moral power (Romola), and amoral manipulation (Tito Melema)."

memes.label: "Power in Romola"
memes.description: "The theme of power — political, ecclesiastical, and personal — as the contested currency of fifteenth-century Florence in George Eliot's Romola."

memes.label: "Prato"
memes.description: "Prato, the Tuscan town near Florence, referenced in the novel's geographic and political landscape of Renaissance Tuscany."

memes.label: "Pride in Romola"
memes.description: "The theme of pride — intellectual, familial, and personal — as a moral and psychological force in George Eliot's Romola, where pride elevates and isolates in equal measure."

memes.label: "Prophecy in Romola"
memes.description: "The role of prophecy — both as religious claim and political instrument — in Savonarola's dominance over Florence and in the moral unfolding of George Eliot's Romola."

memes.label: "Religious Faith in Romola"
memes.description: "The theme of religious faith — particularly the Savonarolan Christianity that challenges and ultimately transforms the novel's pagan-classical worldview."

memes.label: "Renaissance Humanism"
memes.description: "The intellectual movement of classical revival that dominated fifteenth-century Florence, embodied in the novel by scholars like Bardo and the early promise of Tito Melema."

memes.label: "Romola de' Bardi"
memes.description: "The novel's heroine: a Florentine noblewoman, scholar, and moral compass whose journey from intellectual idealism to active compassion forms the spiritual heart of the narrative."

memes.label: "Sacrifice in Romola"
memes.description: "The theme of sacrifice — self-giving, renunciation, and the moral logic of putting others before oneself — as the novel's highest ethical ideal in George Eliot's Romola."

memes.label: "San Lorenzo"
memes.description: "The Basilica of San Lorenzo, the Medici family church and the pulpit from which Savonarola delivered his fiery Lenten sermons."

memes.label: "San Marco"
memes.description: "The Dominican Convent of San Marco in Florence, home to Fra Girolamo Savonarola and Fra Luca (Dino de' Bardi), and the spiritual centre of the novel's religious crisis."

memes.label: "Convent of San Marco"
memes.description: "The Dominican Convent of San Marco in Florence — the institutional base of Girolamo Savonarola's reform movement and a centre of religious and political power in George Eliot's Romola."

memes.label: "Santa Croce"
memes.description: "The Franciscan Basilica of Santa Croce, burial place of Florence's distinguished dead, and a key location for Romola's personal and spiritual journey."

memes.label: "Ponte Santa Trinita"
memes.description: "The Ponte Santa Trinita, the elegant Arno bridge whose crossing marks crucial moments of flight and pursuit in the novel's final movement."

memes.label: "Girolamo Savonarola"
memes.description: "Girolamo Savonarola (1452–1498), Dominican friar and Prior of San Marco, whose prophetic preaching and moral reform movement dominated Florentine politics during the period of the novel."

memes.label: "The Savonarola Era in Romola"
memes.description: "How George Eliot uses the historical Savonarola period (1494–1498) to explore the collision between religious conviction and political power, and what the failure of Savonarola's Florence reveals about the limits of prophetic authority."

memes.label: Savonarola Era
memes.description: Pages related to the Savonarola period in the Romola wiki, including the Bonfire of the Vanities and the Dominican reform movement.

memes.label: "Excommunication of Savonarola"
memes.description: "The papal excommunication of Savonarola by Pope Alexander VI in 1497, which silenced the Frate's preaching and set in motion his political isolation and eventual downfall."

memes.label: "Tabernacle of the Crucifix"
memes.description: "A small painted tabernacle by Piero di Cosimo, given by Tito Melema to Tessa, which housed Dino de' Bardi's ivory crucifix — a recurring symbolic artwork in Romola."

memes.label: "Tessa"
memes.description: "A simple, innocent Florentine contadina (milk-seller) who becomes Tito Melema's secret mistress and the mother of his children."

memes.label: "Tito Melema"
memes.description: "A young Greek scholar who arrives in Florence after a shipwreck, becomes the novel's central figure of intellectual brilliance and moral decay."

memes.label: "Trial by Fire"
memes.description: "The Trial by Fire of April 1498 — Savonarola's proposed ordeal that failed to take place, precipitating his arrest and execution."

memes.label: "Trial of Savonarola"
memes.description: "The arrest, trial, and execution of Girolamo Savonarola in May 1498 — the dramatic end of Florence's theocratic republic and a pivotal narrative climax."

memes.label: Works Index
memes.description: Index of all works referenced in the Romola wiki — art, manuscripts, and literary works from the Renaissance.
